JP/Tokyo.cultural_notes: Formality matters. Bowing depth matters. Business cards with both hands.

slang_era.gen_z: 草 (kusa=lol), ぴえん (pien=sad), エモい (emoi), やばい (yabai=amazing/terrible)
slang_era.millennial: マジで (maji de=seriously), ウケる (ukeru=funny), 推し (oshi=fave)
slang_era.older: ございます forms, しょうがない (shouganai=it can't be helped)

JP/Osaka.cultural_notes: Direct, humorous, food-obsessed. なんでやねん is quintessential.

slang_era.gen_z: めっちゃ (meccha=very), あかんて (akante=no way)
slang_era.millennial: なんでやねん, ほんまに (honmani=really), あかん (akan=no good)
slang_era.older: おおきに (ookini=thank you), あきまへん (akimahen=no good)

VN/Ho Chi Minh City.cultural_notes: Called Saigon by locals. Casual vibe. Coffee culture central.

slang_era.older: Formal pronouns (ông/bà), traditional politeness, indirect speech

FR/Paris.cultural_notes: ALWAYS start with Bonjour. Vous before tu. Effort in French appreciated.

slang_era.gen_z: C'est chaud (intense), la flemme (can't be bothered), sah (fr fr)
slang_era.millennial: kiffer (to love), grave (totally), ouf (crazy, verlan of fou)
slang_era.older: Literary tenses, proper subjunctive, fewer anglicisms

MX/Mexico City.cultural_notes: Extremely friendly. Diminutives everywhere. Güey=dude. Ahorita=soon not now.

slang_era.gen_z: neta (fr fr), nmms (no mames), cringe (English loan)
slang_era.older: Usted more common, formal diminutives, fewer anglicisms

KR/Seoul.cultural_notes: Age hierarchy is everything. Ask age early — determines speech level.

NP/Kathmandu.cultural_notes: Kathmandu Valley — Hindu/Buddhist mix, young urban population, Hindi film influence, English common in educated circles. Namaste (नमस्ते) with hands folded is universal. Write phrases in both Devanagari script and romanized transliteration.

slang_era.gen_z: यार (yaar=friend), छ (chha=is/yes), लु (lu=ok/alright), झैं (jhain=like/similar to), किन गर्छस् (kin garcchas=why do you do this)
slang_era.millennial: हजुर (hajur=yes/respect), ठीक छ (thik chha=ok), क्या हो (kya ho=what's up), मिल्यो (milyo=got it/found it)
slang_era.older: Formal honorifics हजुर, सेवामा (sewama=at your service), indirect speech, classical Nepali terms
